generated_from: UI refinement discussion covering note view, thinking panel, graph interaction, organization controls, relationship management, deletion model, and default/home experience
guiding_principles: Content first, controls second, structure third
The system should feel calm, legible, and trustworthy
The user should always know where to read, where to type, and what happens next
Everything does not need to be hidden, but everything needs rank
Graph and note should feel like one spatial system, not competing layers
Use progressive disclosure rather than persistent explanation
Primary UI should use plain language, not internal abbreviations or initialisms

epics.title: Reduce note view to a minimal, hierarchy-first reading experience
epics.goal: Make open notes feel quiet, legible, and intentional by prioritizing reading and demoting secondary controls.

stories.title: Move note tools to a bottom toolbar
stories.user_story: As a user opening a note, I want note tools to live at the bottom so the content is visually primary and the note feels calmer.
stories.description: Relocate note-level actions from top and corner emphasis areas into a footer toolbar within the note shell. The footer should contain only the most common note actions.
acceptance_criteria: The note header contains title, mode, and only minimal metadata/actions.
The note body is the most visually dominant area in both Read and Edit modes.
Primary note actions are rendered in a footer toolbar below the content.
No floating or corner control visually outweighs the note body.

stories.title: Simplify Read view to remove persistent visual competition
stories.user_story: As a user in Read view, I want the interface to stop competing with the note so I can understand the note immediately.
stories.description: Reduce visible non-essential elements in Read mode. Keep note content, minimal metadata, and a subtle footer toolbar. Hide or collapse secondary panels by default.
acceptance_criteria: Read view shows note content first with no large explanatory blocks around it.
AI prompts, organization controls, and relationship panels are hidden or collapsed by default.
A user can identify where to read within 3 seconds of opening the note.
A user can distinguish primary content from optional controls at a glance.

stories.title: Make workspace assignment directly clickable from the note header
stories.user_story: As a user, I want the workspace status in the note header to be actionable so it feels intentional and consistent with other note actions.
stories.description: Replace inert workspace status text with a clickable chip or pill in both assigned and unassigned states.
acceptance_criteria: Unassigned state renders as a clear action such as 'Assign workspace'.
Assigned state renders as a clickable workspace chip such as 'Workspace: Data'.
The control matches the interaction style of adjacent note actions like Focus and Archive.
Clicking the control opens workspace selection without requiring the user to hunt elsewhere.

stories.title: Convert organization sections into progressive disclosures
stories.user_story: As a user, I want Anchored In and Shared Threads to stay out of the way until I need them.
stories.description: Turn always-expanded organization panels into compact disclosure sections with collapsed summaries.
acceptance_criteria: Anchored In is collapsed by default in Read view.
Shared Threads is collapsed by default in Read view.
Collapsed state shows concise summaries such as current workspace and linked thread count.
Expanding these sections does not resize unrelated canvas regions.

stories.title: Remove clipped corner arrow from the note shell
stories.user_story: As a user, I want the note shell to feel polished, without clipped or awkward corner controls.
stories.description: Eliminate the current arrow control from the note corner and rehome any related behavior to a stable navigation location outside the card.
acceptance_criteria: No clipped or partially inset arrow remains in the note corner.
The note shell corners render cleanly at all supported sizes.
Any displaced navigation behavior remains accessible elsewhere in the interface.

stories.title: Remove unexplained initialisms from visible note UI
stories.user_story: As a user, I want visible labels to use plain language so the product feels polished and self-explanatory.
stories.description: Replace initialisms and prototype-style abbreviations in note-visible UI with readable labels.
acceptance_criteria: No unexplained initialisms appear in primary note UI.
Shared thread labels show readable names rather than internal abbreviations.
Visible terminology is consistent across note, side panels, and related controls.

epics.title: Rebuild the Thinking panel into a safe, obvious interaction surface
epics.goal: Make the Thinking panel feel usable, calm, and unmistakably interactive by establishing a clear place to type and reducing preloaded explanation.

stories.title: Create a single obvious input at the top of Thinking
stories.user_story: As a user, I want to immediately know where to type when I open Thinking.
stories.description: Add a top-level input field with clear placeholder text. The input becomes the primary focus target for the panel.
acceptance_criteria: The first visible interactive element in Thinking is a text input.
The placeholder clearly invites action, for example 'Ask about this note or your work...'.
A user can identify where to type without reading surrounding copy.
Input focus behavior is predictable on open and accessible by keyboard.

stories.title: Reduce Thinking to shortcuts plus results-after-action
stories.user_story: As a user, I want Thinking to present a clear next action rather than a wall of explanation and preloaded content.
stories.description: Strip explanatory copy and preloaded result blocks from the default panel state. Present only the input and a few shortcut actions. Render results only after the user acts.
acceptance_criteria: Default Thinking state contains the input and no more than three optional shortcut actions.
There are no large explanatory paragraphs in the default state.
Preloaded result cards do not render before user interaction.
Results appear below the input only after the user types, selects a shortcut, or submits a request.

stories.title: Replace internal or developer-like language in Thinking
stories.user_story: As a user, I want the Thinking panel to speak plainly instead of explaining itself with system terminology.
stories.description: Remove copy such as 'graph-aware, note-aware, and action-oriented' and replace it with short human language or no copy at all.
acceptance_criteria: No system-self-descriptive jargon appears in the default panel state.
Any remaining helper copy is one line or less and directly supports action.
The panel no longer requires reading explanatory prose before use.

stories.title: Replace the current brain icon with a more legible metaphor
stories.user_story: As a user, I want the Thinking icon to be recognizable and polished.
stories.description: Swap the current brain glyph for a more legible icon aligned to the feature's actual semantics, such as insight, constellation, refinement, or assistance.
acceptance_criteria: The replacement icon is visually distinct and legible at toolbar size.
The icon no longer reads as an ambiguous or malformed brain.
The icon is consistent wherever Thinking appears in the UI.

stories.title: Fix Thinking collapse so it does not resize the entire canvas
stories.user_story: As a user, I want collapsing Thinking to affect only that module so the rest of the workspace feels stable.
stories.description: Decouple Thinking panel expansion and collapse from global canvas height and unrelated layout regions.
acceptance_criteria: Collapsing Thinking does not shrink the entire canvas vertically.
The note body and graph surface remain dimensionally stable.
No layout jump occurs when expanding or collapsing Thinking.

stories.title: Preserve contextual color behavior for Thinking
stories.user_story: As a user, I want Thinking to feel contextually ready when a note is active.
stories.description: Keep and refine the behavior where the panel changes color or accent when a note is opened, reinforcing that Thinking is scoped to current context.
acceptance_criteria: Opening a note changes Thinking panel accenting in a way tied to current context.
The contextual color treatment feels subtle and intentional rather than loud.
The color change does not reduce readability or suggest unrelated state changes.

epics.title: Preserve graph visibility and spatial trust when notes open
epics.goal: Make notes and graph relationships feel like one shared plane so the user does not need to fight layout, dragging, or obscured structure.

stories.title: Keep graph relationships visible when a note opens
stories.user_story: As a user, I want to keep seeing the surrounding graph when I open a note so I do not lose the relationship context I was inspecting.
stories.description: Change note-open behavior so graph relationships remain visible through dimming, anchored expansion, or another shared-plane treatment rather than being fully obscured.
acceptance_criteria: Opening a note does not fully hide the nearby graph context.
At least the most relevant local relationships remain visible while the note is open.
The treatment feels spatially consistent with the graph rather than like a modal covering it.

stories.title: Persist note open position or eliminate the need to reposition
stories.user_story: As a user, I want the system to respect where I move an open note so I do not have to drag it out of the way repeatedly.
stories.description: Remember user-adjusted note position per session or adopt a smarter anchored layout that removes the need for manual repositioning.
acceptance_criteria: If a user repositions an open note, reopening it during the session restores the adjusted position.
If anchored layout is used instead, opening the note does not require manual dragging to reveal graph content.
The user no longer experiences repeated resets to an obstructive default position.

stories.title: Create a shared-plane visual treatment for note plus graph
stories.user_story: As a user, I want note focus and graph context to feel coordinated, not like separate layers fighting for space.
stories.description: Establish a visual model where the active note and its nearby graph context coexist through dimming, soft highlight, or anchored expansion.
acceptance_criteria: The active note feels integrated with the graph rather than floating above it as a blocking layer.
Nearby graph content remains discoverable without overwhelming note readability.
The active note state is visually clear without causing the graph to disappear.

stories.title: Restore missing double-tap interaction control
stories.user_story: As a user, I want double-tap behavior to work consistently so common note and canvas actions feel responsive and complete.
stories.description: Implement or restore the missing double-tap control behavior for the intended note or canvas action. Use the existing product interaction model to determine exact behavior if already defined elsewhere.
acceptance_criteria: Double-tap triggers the intended action reliably on supported targets.
Double-tap does not conflict with single-click, drag, or pointer-up open behavior.
The interaction feels deliberate rather than accidental.
If platform constraints require limits, those limits are documented and consistent.

epics.title: Give the graph gently enforced order without hiding everything
epics.goal: Keep the surface rich and alive while introducing calm hierarchy so the system feels ordered rather than chaotic.

stories.title: Establish ranked visual layers across the workspace
stories.user_story: As a user, I want the interface to tell me what matters most right now without hiding the rest of the system.
stories.description: Define a hierarchy of always-visible content, quiet secondary context, and intent-driven controls. Apply it consistently across note, graph, and panel surfaces.
acceptance_criteria: Primary content surfaces are visually dominant over metadata and controls.
Secondary context such as workspace and thread summaries is visible but visually quieter.
Action-heavy or AI-heavy regions appear only on intent or remain clearly subordinate.
The surface feels ordered without becoming sparse or empty.

stories.title: Create calmer defaults for graph-side assistance and metadata
stories.user_story: As a user, I want the interface to start calm and then become richer only when I ask for it.
stories.description: Default nonessential panels and assistive modules to quiet or collapsed states, while preserving rapid access.
acceptance_criteria: The default workspace state does not present multiple equally loud panels at once.
Hidden or collapsed items remain quickly accessible.
The user can move from calm default to rich exploration without feeling blocked.

epics.title: Support explicit relationship management with trust and clarity
epics.goal: Make it obvious how to remove relationships and preserve confidence in the graph as a user-controlled structure.

stories.title: Expose relationship removal affordance where relationships are shown
stories.user_story: As a user, I want to remove a relationship from the place I can see it so the graph feels controllable.
stories.description: Add a contextual remove affordance to relationship list items, chips, or cards, exposed on hover, selection, or overflow rather than permanently loud.
acceptance_criteria: Each visible explicit relationship has a discoverable remove affordance.
The affordance is contextual and does not clutter the default UI.
A user can remove a relationship from either linked note context or a shared relationship panel.
Relationship removal updates the UI immediately without confusing animation.

stories.title: Differentiate explicit relationship removal from inferred relationship dismissal
stories.user_story: As a user, I want system-suggested links to behave differently from links I created intentionally.
stories.description: Treat explicit relationships as removable edges and inferred relationships as dismissible or ignorable suggestions to avoid reappearing confusion.
acceptance_criteria: Explicit relationships use language such as 'Remove link'.
Inferred relationships use language such as 'Dismiss' or 'Don't suggest again'.
The system does not silently recreate a user-removed explicit edge.
Dismissed inferred suggestions respect the user's intent according to product rules.

epics.title: Implement a safe, reversible deletion model distinct from archive
epics.goal: Clarify archive versus delete so users can manage notes confidently without fearing accidental loss of structure.

stories.title: Separate Archive and Delete into clearly named actions
stories.user_story: As a user, I want Archive and Delete to be distinct actions so I understand the consequence before I click.
stories.description: Present archive as the reversible, everyday cleanup action and delete as a separate destructive action with distinct placement and language.
acceptance_criteria: Archive and Delete are shown as separate actions with distinct labels.
Archive is described as reversible and suitable for hiding notes from the active canvas.
Delete is visually subordinate until intentionally invoked.
The UI never implies that Archive permanently removes a note.

stories.title: Add a deletion confirmation with structure impact summary
stories.user_story: As a user, I want to understand what else will be affected before a note is permanently deleted.
stories.description: Before deletion, show a concise confirmation state that summarizes linked relationships, project/workspace membership, and whether recovery is still possible for a short window.
acceptance_criteria: Delete requires an explicit confirmation step.
The confirmation summarizes affected links or structural context in plain language.
The confirmation does not overwhelm the user with raw metadata.
If undo is supported, the recovery window is stated clearly.

stories.title: Preserve a calm undo path for destructive actions
stories.user_story: As a user, I want one last easy way back after deleting so I can act confidently without fearing irreversible mistakes.
stories.description: Provide a short-lived undo or recovery affordance after delete that is clear, unobtrusive, and consistent with the product's trust model.
acceptance_criteria: After deletion, the UI provides a clear but quiet undo path when product policy allows it.
Undo feedback appears near the active workflow rather than as a disruptive modal chain.
Expired undo states are communicated plainly.
The deletion model stays distinct from Archive at every step.

epics.title: Refine the default and home experience into a clear next-step surface
epics.goal: Make the first view feel immediately useful by presenting capture, recent momentum, and likely next actions without turning the home surface into a dashboard.

stories.title: Make the home surface answer 'Where should I start?'
stories.user_story: As a user returning to the app, I want the default view to quickly orient me toward my next meaningful action.
stories.description: Refine the home surface so its primary message, capture area, and recent work sections clearly answer what to do now.
acceptance_criteria: The default home state communicates a clear first action or next step.
Capture remains immediately available without dominating everything else.
Recent and active work are easy to scan at a glance.
The home surface feels like orientation, not a dashboard overload.

stories.title: Rank home sections by usefulness rather than equal visual weight
stories.user_story: As a user, I want the most relevant recent work to stand out so I can resume momentum without scanning noisy lists.
stories.description: Introduce stronger visual hierarchy across home modules so recent work, open tasks, and useful context do not all compete equally.
acceptance_criteria: The most relevant home section is visually primary.
Secondary summaries remain available but quieter.
Lists are short, ranked, and easy to scan.
The surface preserves calmness while still feeling informative.

stories.title: Use plain-language empty and onboarding states on home
stories.user_story: As a user new to the space, I want the default experience to guide me with plain language instead of product jargon.
stories.description: Rewrite empty and early-use states so they explain capture, recent work, and next steps in human language with minimal copy.
acceptance_criteria: Home empty states use plain language and avoid internal terminology.
Helper text is brief and action-oriented.
A new user can tell where to type and what happens next without reading a long explanation.
Onboarding cues remain visually subordinate to actual actions.
